coredata.dc:description: Photocatalysis is one of the most preferred methods for the degradation of organic pollutants as it can lead to complete mineralization of organic pollutants employing sunlight as an energy source. Until so far enormous number of investigations have been done regarding the development of visible light responsive, stable, and cost effective photocatalysts. In the present work, owing to objectives mentioned above, an attempt was made to develop an efficient, cheaper, and visible light active composite photocatalyst consisting of moderate band gap, CuWO4 with electron conductive graphene quantum dots. A series of photocatalysts was prepared by varying the amount of CuWO4 while keeping the amount of GQDs fixed, and their activity was evaluated for the phenol degradation under visible light irradiation. The prepared photocatalysts were characterized by using X-ray diffraction (XRD), Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, and Photoluminescence (PL) for the evaluation of crystallinity, functional groups, and properties of charge carrier separation, respectively. The maximum efficiency of photodegradation of phenol simulated wastewater was achieved by sample 0.5GCW (optimized sample having 0.5 wt% of CuWO4 with respect to fixed amount of GQDs), 53.41%, as compared to the pure CuWO4 sample, which exhibited 19.08% efficiency. A possible mechanism for enhanced activity of CuWO4 is proposed mainly due to efficient transfer of electrons from CuWO4 to graphene quantum dots. The results show that the graphene quantum dots GQDs with the CuWO4 significantly contributes to the improvement of photocatalytic performance.

head.ce:title: Copper tungstate (CuWO4)/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) composite photocatalyst for enhanced degradation of phenol under visible light irradiation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Photocatalysis is one of the most preferred methods for the degradation of organic pollutants as it can lead to complete mineralization of organic pollutants employing sunlight as an energy source. Until so far enormous number of investigations have been done regarding the development of visible light responsive, stable, and cost effective photocatalysts. In the present work, owing to objectives mentioned above, an attempt was made to develop an efficient, cheaper, and visible light active composite photocatalyst consisting of moderate band gap, CuWO4 with electron conductive graphene quantum dots. A series of photocatalysts was prepared by varying the amount of CuWO4 while keeping the amount of GQDs fixed, and their activity was evaluated for the phenol degradation under visible light irradiation. The prepared photocatalysts were characterized by using X-ray diffraction (XRD), Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, and Photoluminescence (PL) for the evaluation of crystallinity, functional groups, and properties of charge carrier separation, respectively. The maximum efficiency of photodegradation of phenol simulated wastewater was achieved by sample 0.5GCW (optimized sample having 0.5 wt% of CuWO4 with respect to fixed amount of GQDs), 53.41%, as compared to the pure CuWO4 sample, which exhibited 19.08% efficiency. A possible mechanism for enhanced activity of CuWO4 is proposed mainly due to efficient transfer of electrons from CuWO4 to graphene quantum dots. The results show that the graphene quantum dots GQDs with the CuWO4 significantly contributes to the improvement of photocatalytic performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution, particularly water pollution, has become a significant issue in recent years. There is an increase in toxicity levels in aquatic environments because of increasing population, changed lifestyle, rapid growth, industrialization, and advancements in agricultural practices [1,2]. The increasing concerns regarding water pollution are due to the increased contamination of water bodies polluted by the discharge of effluents from different industries. The major effluents are related to industrial, agriculture and municipal waste, which are responsible for a large volume of contamination [3–5]. The major pollutants include recalcitrant organic pollutants, pharmaceuticals, pesticides and personal care products. The refractory nature of the pollutants is responsible for causing increased risk to terrestrial and aquatic life, even if it is detected in trace concentrations [6–8].

Phenol and its derivatives are common contaminants in wastewater, originating specifically from petroleum refineries, plastic industries, resin manufacturing, dyes and textile industries, and paper & pulp industries [9–11]. Owing to their recalcitrance, toxicity, and high stability in water, they can cause serious environmental and health problems [12–15]. Phenol is a colorless, highly poisonous, stable, non-biodegradable chemical and acidic when dissolved in the water, posing threats to human health and a major environmental pollutant [16–18]. Phenol and its derivatives are found in various industrial effluents in concentrations of >1 mg/L which is very difficult to decrease to standard levels of 0.1–1 mg/L due to their persistence and solubility in water [19].

For the removal of phenol from wastewater, various technologies including chlorination, adsorption, coagulation, biological degradation, and filtration have been employed [20,21]. Among them, chemical precipitation has been used widely. Such methods have several drawbacks, such as energy requirement, and conversion of pollutants to other phases instead of complete eradication [22,23]. For instance, the by-products of chlorination are carcinogenic and mutagenic compounds [24–26]. Currently, the elimination of these contaminants has become a challenging task because of their increased resistance to chemical and biological treatment in an aqueous medium [27]. Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have been considered as an efficient mean for the removal of organic pollutants [28,29]. The operation of AOPs is based on a simple principle despite the variety of reaction systems, such as the use of hydroxyl radicals as an oxidizing species for the elimination of water pollutants [30–32]. Amongst the AOPs, photocatalytic degradation of pollutants has received a wider attention during last decades because of its importance in harvesting light energy, a free source of energy and a sustainable remediation [33–35]. Photocatalysis primarily employ a semiconductor material as photocatalyst for decomposition of organic molecules by utilization of light energy on semiconductor catalysts surface into mainly carbon dioxide and water [25,36,37] . The process of photocatalysis is based on the generation of reactive oxygen species, including hydroxyl ion, hydrogen peroxide, free electrons, and superoxide radicals [37,38]. The estimation of the degradation efficiency of a photocatalyst can be checked by the edge position of valence and conduction band [39]. The key challenges faced in the domain of photocatalysis is development of an efficient photocatalytic material, possessing enhanced optical absorbance, excellent textural properties and efficient photogenerated charges separation [40]. Until so far semiconductor materials composed of binary oxides, such as WO3, TiO2, Fe2O3, and ZnO, etc., have been extensively studied as photocatalysts due to their better aspects of chemical stability, non-toxicity, strong oxidative ability, and cost effectiveness etc [41,42]. Due to such characteristic and material properties, these semiconductors are usually selected for photocatalytic process, however oxides a potential barrier is the limited light absorption due to wider band gap, for instance, the band gap of TiO2 is approximately 3.2 eV, thus limiting its light absorption to ultraviolet (UV) region of sunlight [43–46]. To overcome such a limitation of restricted light absorption, certain strategies such as metals and non-metals doping [47–49], heterojunction photocatalysts [50–52], compositing with conductive substrates [53,54], and noble metals loading [55,56] . However, to develop an alternative oxide semiconductor can also result in an efficient approach for enhanced photocatalytic performance. Amongst various moderate band gap semiconductors, copper tungstate (CuWO4) is a potential n-type semiconductor photocatalyst [57], having a band gap of 2.2 eV [58,59]. The position of the valance band is highly positive on the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) scale resulting in the generation of photo-excited holes having strong oxidation ability. Furthermore, under neutral conditions, CuWO4 is stable against photo-corrosion. Since the conduction band position is in a positive region on the NHE scale, therefore, improvement in the structure is required for sufficient reducing ability [60].

To enhance the charge separation efficiency, decoration of the photocatalyst material with metal nanoparticles is one of the most popular strategies. The commonly applied metal nanoparticles because of their unique catalytic characteristics, for the decoration of the photocatalyst include platinum (Pt) [55,56,61], palladium (Pd) [62], gold (Au) [63], and some alloys [64]. These metals are used as electron sinks or reservoirs, spatially separating electrons from the photoexcited holes in the semiconductor's valence bond, providing a more efficient active site for the continuation of the photocatalytic reduction reaction [65]. Despite of better charge separation efficiency, the problem with the use of these metals is their high cost. Therefore, cheap carbon-based materials are being exploited to overcome this issue without compromising the efficiency of the system. The materials based on graphene (one layer of sp2 bonded carbon in a honeycomb lattice, or basically, one/single layer of graphite) are widely investigated because of special properties such as increased optical transparency, zero band gap, high carrier mobility and high surface area. With the development of graphene derivatives in photocatalysis, i.e., graphene oxide (GO) [66] and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [53,67], a very attractive type of graphene structure named graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have drawn the attention of researchers [68–70]. The modification of electron behavior because of quantum confinement is one of the main reasons that differentiate the graphene quantum structures from the graphene. Quantum confinement effects become more evident after the reduction in the size of graphene in comparison to the exciton Bohr radius. The electrical and optical properties of graphene quantum structure are affected by the size, shape and edge structure [71].

Hence being inspired form the properties of GQDs and selecting CuWO4 as a potential moderate band gap oxide photocatalyst, an attempt has been done aimed towards the development of GQDs/ CuWO4 composite photocatalysts with an efficient photocatalytic performance. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) used with CuWO4 to make a composite because of their unique electronic and optical properties induced by quantum confinement and edge effect [72]. Pure CuWO4 was synthesized by simple co-precipitation method, and composite was fabricated by a simple mixing of CuWO4 and GQDs solution in a hydrothermal reactor. The photocatalytic performance of the prepared samples was evaluated by their tendency to degrade phenol simulated wastewater.
Sodium tungstate dihydrate (Na2WO4·2H2O, ACS Reagent ≥ 99 %), copper sulfate.

pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O, ≥ 98 %), and Sodium hydroxide platelets (NaOH, ≥ 98 %) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and used without any modification. Citric acid (C6H8O7, ≥ 99.5 %) was purchased from Panreac Quimica SLU, ITW Reagents. Phenol (C6H6O, % in weight > 99 %) was purchased from Penta Chemicals and used as a simulated pollutant in wastewater. Deionized water used throughout the experiments was produced by Aqua Flow (Taiwan) reverse osmosis water purifier system. For reactive species investigation, Iso propyl alcohol (IPA, 2-Propanol, ACS reagent, > 99.5 %, Sigma Aldrich) and sodium iodide (NaI, ACS reagent, > 99.5 %, Sigma Aldrich) were used without any modification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of pure copper tungstate, CuWO4
ce:section.ce:para: Pure CuWO4 was synthesized by a co-precipitation method [73]. Briefly, solution A (50.0 ml) of 0.4 M Na2WO4·2H2O in deionized water, and solution B (50.0 ml) of 0.4 M CuSO4·2.5H2O were prepared. Solution A was added dropwise into solution B, at ambient temperature (28 °C), resulting in spontaneous formation of precipitates. The precipitated suspension was kept under vigorous stirring for 2 h, followed by vacuum filtration, washing, and drying in oven (100 °C for 24 h). The sample was ground in an agitate mortar and calcined for 4 h at 400 ℃.

Graphene quantum dots were prepared by carbonization of citric acid [74]. 2 g of citric acid was heated at 200 ℃ for 30 min. The white color of citric acid was turned orange red, indicating the successful synthesis of GQDs. The melted orange liquid was dissolved in 100 ml NaOH solution (10 mg/ml).As a result the concentration of GQDs 0.0226 mol/L was obtained.

For the synthesis of composite photocatalysts, varied amount of synthesized CuWO4: 0.3 g, 0.5 g, and 0.7 g are added separately into 60 ml of the already prepared GQDs solution. The suspension is then stirred vigorously for 1 h, transferred to 100 ml hydrothermal reactor with Teflon chamber and kept at temperature of 120 ℃ in an oven for 7 h. After the hydrothermal process, the product is allowed to cool naturally to ambient temperature, taken out and washed with DI water, centrifuged and oven dried. The composite samples of CuWO4 with fixed vol. of GQDs, are labeled as xGCW, where x = 0.3, 0.5 and 0.7 g of pure CuWO4 in fixed vol. of GQDs (60 ml), thus representing as 0.3GCW, 0.5GCW and 0.7GCW respectively.

The crystalline nature of the prepared photocatalysts was measured using X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy (PANalytical X-pert Powder diffractometer, Pro DY38059) employing Cu Kα radiations of 0.15406 nm. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometry was performed to investigate the functional groups within prepared samples. FTIR spectra was measured using a PerkinElmer spectrometer in ATR mode within the range from 4000 cm−1 to 650 cm−1. Raman and photoluminescence (PL) were examined with the laser excitation source of 514 nm and 457 nm, respectively, by RENISHAW UK. For optical absorbance measurement, GQDs, and a dilute suspension of pure CuWO4 and 0.5GCW were subjected to UV Vis spectroscopy using UV-1602 spectrometer provided by Biotechnology Medical Services (BMS). The band gap (Eg) values for the synthesized photocatalysts were estimated using Tauc’s method, employing relation (αhν) n = A(hν-Eg), with consideration of direct band gap i.e. n = 2 [75–79]. The linear portion of the curves were extended toward the zero line of x-axis, estimating the band gap value at the point of intersection of x-intercept.

The photocatalytic performance of pure and composite photocatalysts was assessed by the degradation capability of phenol contaminated simulated water under visible light irradiation. Briefly, 0.2 g of the prepared photocatalyst i.e., pure CuWO4, 0.3GCW, 0.5GCW and 0.7GCW were separately added into 100 ml pf phenol solution (100 mg/l) and allowed to stir under dark for 1 h to attain adsorption–desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst surface and phenol pollutant. After the completion of dark stirring, 5 W LED light acting as a source of visible light was turned on, and sample (3.0 ml) from the beaker was taken after every 10 min., centrifuged for 5 min at 4500 rpm, and phenol concentration measured using UV–Visible absorption spectrometer (UV-1602, Biotechnology Medical Services). The degradation efficiency for the phenol was calculated by the following equation [49,66]:
D e g r a d a t i o n

The reactive species analysis was also performed by addition of IPA, and NaI in the phenol solution before the addition of photocatalysts and photocatalytic reaction. The degradation experiments were performed in similar manner as for photocatalytic activity test and phenol degradation was measured using UV–Visible absorption spectroscopy. The concentration of both IPA and NaI was kept 5 mM in phenol solution.

The crystallinity and phase identification of pure CuWO4, GQDs, and the composite photocatalysts xGCW (where x = 0.3, 0.5 and 0.7 g of pure CuWO4), was carried out by obtaining respective X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, shown in Fig. 1 . It can be observed that pure CuWO4 sample exhibits characteristic XRD peaks appearing at 2θ values of 15.82°, 19.56°, 29.24°, 30.06°, 31.36°, 32.01°, and 56°, corresponding to (010), (100), (111), (111), (020), (111), (111) planes [60]. After thermal oxidation, according to Inorganic Crystal Structure Database (ICSD) the characteristic peak of Cu(II) binary oxide (CuO) can be observed around 36°[80]. Thus, all the characteristic peaks appearing assures the prepared CuWO4 sample possesses a triclinic crystalline structure as reported [81]. According to the ICSD-29123 the XRD pattern for GQDs show a characteristic peak at 2θ = 27.74°, which can be assigned to the characteristic (002) graphitic plane [82,83]. All composite photocatalyst samples: 0.3GCW, 0.5GCW, and 0.7GCW, exhibit similar characteristics peaks as observed for pure CuWO4 sample, indicating all the composite samples possesses triclinic structure. The composite samples show a very small graphitic peak due to GQDs in respective XRD pattern, this might be due to the reason of low graphitic content or masking of peak due to CuWO4 peaks.

Further investigation of regarding the crystallinity and phase was performed by obtaining the Raman spectra of the prepared samples. Fig. 2 a shows the Raman spectrum of GQDs displaying the characteristic peaks appearing at 1282 cm−1 and 1471 cm−1 assigned to D and G bands, respectively. The D band is related to the carbon atom vibrations with dangling bonds of the distorted carbon structure, whereas the G band is assigned to the E2g phonon mode of sp2 carbon atoms vibration in a 2D hexagonal lattice of graphite clusters.

Fig. 2b shows the Raman spectra for the pure CuWO4 and composite photocatalyst samples. Tungstates with the triclinic structure are reported to show 36 different (infrared and Raman) vibrational modes (according to the calculations of group theory) presented by the following equation:
Γ

Where Ag indicates, Raman active vibrational modes and Au indicates Infrared vibrational modes. 18 Raman active vibrational modes are estimated, as can be seen in Fig. 2b for the triclinic CuWO4 [84].

A g

As well-established by reported literature, the vibrational modes of tungsten materials are characterized into two groups external and internal modes. Lattice phonons are related to the external vibrational modes, which are due to the motion of distorted octahedral [CuO6] clusters. The internal vibrational modes are related to the vibrations of distorted octahedral [WO6] clusters in the lattice [85,86]. The sharp Raman peaks are observed for both pure CuWO4 and composite photocatalysts samples appearing around 220 cm−1, 780 cm−1, and 905 cm−1. The peak at 780 cm−1 is attributed to asymmetric vibrations of W-O bonds whereas the peak at 905 cm−1 is related to the symmetric stretching vibration of W-O bonds [87,88]. The peaks at 355 and 395 cm−1 are assigned to the deformation modes of WO2 [36,89]. Hence the Raman spectra further assured the crystalline nature of the prepared samples [90].

Morphological analysis for the pure CuWO4 and 0.5GCW (representative composite sample) is done by obtaining scanning electron microscopy images shown in Fig. 4
. It can be observed SEM image of pure CuWO4 (Fig. 3a) sample shows aggregated uniformly packed nanoparticles of somewhat dendritic nature. However, upon addition of GQDs, and formation of a composite photocatalyst, no significant difference in the morphology is observed (Fig. 3b). This might be due to ultra-small size of GQDs and/or sheet like graphitic nature well composted with the CuWO4 nanoparticles.

The functional groups identification and bonding composition of the prepared samples was investigated by FTIR spectroscopy. Fig. 4a displays FTIR spectra of GQDs exhibiting characteristic stretching peak of CC appearing around 1635 cm 1, confirming the formation of graphene quantum dots by complete dehydration of citric acid. A weak CH2 vibration from precursors emerges at 840 cm−1 [91]. The peak observed at 1480 cm−1 is assigned to the CH bond, which might be due to incomplete carbonization of citric acid [92]. The functional group COC peak is also observed at 1270 cm−1 present at the surface of GQDs. The stretching vibration within range of 3100–3500 cm−1 is attributed to the surface/adsorbed water molecules aiding the solubility of GQDs [93].

FTIR spectra for pure CuWO4, and composite photocatalyst samples are shown in Fig. 4b. It is obvious that all samples exhibit peaks around 7745 cm−1 and 910 cm−1, attributed to stretching modes of Cu-O and W-O bonds, respectively [76,94]. Moreover, it can be seen that all the composite samples i.e., 0.3GCW, 0.5GCW and 0.7GCW, depicts the characteristic stretching mode of CC, appearing around 1550 cm−1, thus to a great extent confirming the interaction/incorporation of GQDs with CuWO4 in the composite photocatalyst samples.

The efficient separation of photoexcited charges within the photocatalysts prepared was investigated by PL spectroscopy. It is well-established that was intensity of the emission peaks provide a meaningful data regarding the charge generation and separation. Fig. 5 a shows the PL spectra for the GQDs solution excited by laser with wavelength of 457 nm. With an excitation wavelength of 457 nm, the PL emission peak is observed at 583 nm. The PL spectra for graphene quantum dots are usually obtained in the blue-green region by an excitation source of 375 nm or below. However due to limitation of laser source and utilization of laser source of 457 nm, the PL peak for graphene quantum dots shifts to 583 nm. It is reported that the shifting of emission peak takes place with the increasing excitation laser (source) wavelength [93,95]. The intensity of emission wavelength is dependent on the wavelength of the excitation source; this is regarded as a common phenomenon in carbon-based quantum dots caused by free zigzagging sites, electronic conjugates structures, and emissive traps [96].

The PL spectra of pure CuWO4 and composited photocatalysts samples (0.3GCW, 0.5GCW, and 0.7GCW) are shown in Fig. 5b. For all the samples, the PL emission peaks can be sectioned into two regions;(i) firstly the peak appearing at 708 nm, and (ii) a broader humpy coupled peaks appearing after 750 nm to onwards. The peak appearing at 708 nm is attributed to the electronic transition in WO4 -2 anion, which is coupled with an intrinsic emission. The lowering of this peak intensity is observed for composite samples; 0.3GCW, 0.5GCW, and 0.7GCW as compared to pure CuWO4, which represents the improved photogenerated charge separation and/or inhibition of charge recombination in the composite samples [60]. Such phenomenon can be attributed to the coupling of CuWO4 and GQDs, where GQDs substrate can act as an electron extractor, providing a conductive pathway for photogenerated charges to separate out efficiently before recombination, ultimately enhancing the photocatalytic performance.

For composite photocatalyst samples, another broader humpy peak is observed around 750 nm to onwards. The intensity of this peak is increased with increased amount of GQDs (decreased with increased amount of CuWO4 in composite samples) hence providing a clue regarding the emissions induced by the defect states due to incorporation of GQDs into CuWO4. However, these defect states can increase the efficiency of composite photocatalysts by trapping electrons and preventing the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes [97].

For the investigation of the optical absorbance, UV Vis absorption spectra of GQDs solution and, uniformly prepared suspension of pure CuWO4 and 0.5GCW (representative composite sample with best photocatalytic activity) was obtained. Fig. 6 a display the UV Vis absorption spectrum for GQDs solution exhibiting absorption onsets at wavelengths of 235 nm and 320 nm. The absorption obtained at 235 nm can be ascribed to π-π* transition of graphitic sp2 domains [98,99], whereas the absorption obtained around 320 nm can be associated to n-π* absorption [100]. Fig. 6b shows the UV–visible absorption spectra of uniformly dispersed photocatalysts; pure CuWO4 and all composite samples i.e., x-GCW. It is observed that 0.3 GCW samples exhibits red shift and enhanced absorbance as compared to pure CuWO4. Such an enhancement can be attributed to a moderate amount of GQDs (as CuWO4 amount is less in this sample) samples promoting a better composite with a possibility of to some extent of doping. For the other two composite samples i.e., 0.5 GCW and 0.7 GCW, no significant difference in terms of absorption peak shifting as compared to pure CuWO4, however an increase in absorbance can be observed within the visible range (500–800 nm). Such an increase in absorbance for both composite samples as compared to pure CuWO4 can be attributed to graphitic content of GQDs thus providing a clue for the presence of GQDs in the composite sample.

The band gaps for pure CuWO4 and x-GCW samples were estimated using Tauc’s relationship and shown in Fig. 7 . The value of the band gap (Eg) can be obtained by using the Tauc’s relation, (αhν) n = A(hν-Eg), with consideration of direct band gap i.e. n = 2 [75–79]. The estimated band gap of CuWO4, 0.5GCW, and 0.7GCW were 2.32 eV, 2.39 eV, and 2.35 eV respectively. For the sample 0.3GCW sample exhibit with band gap value of 2.3 eV with an additional slope (x-axis intercept in between 2.0 and 2.1 eV) suggesting a good interaction of GQDs with CuWO4 resulting in moderate level of surface and/or core defects.

The photocatalytic performance evaluation of the prepared photocatalysts is investigated by photodegradation of phenol simulated wastewater under visible light (5-watt LED light) irradiation. The degradation concentration profile and % degradation efficiency are shown in Fig. 8 . A decrease in phenol concentration is observed under dark condition (60 min), which is mainly attributed to adsorption process. Such an adsorption under dark is intended to obtain adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Upon light irradiation, further decrease in phenol concentration is obtained which is associated to photocatalytic degradation of phenol on the surface of the photocatalysts.

The degradation efficiency (%) curves shown in Fig. 8b exhibits that pure CuWO4 display 19.08 % degradation. On contrary the composite samples exhibit enhanced degradation as compared to pure CuWO4. Among composite samples, 0.5GCW samples displayed the best photocatalytic degradation, up to 53.41 %, 2.8 times over that of CuWO4, while 0.3GCW and 0.7GCW composites showed 22.53 % and 41.5 % efficiency, respectively. The photocatalytic performance in terms of phenol degradation can be ordered as: 0.5GCW > 0.7GCW > 0.3GCW > CuWO4.

The rate of the reaction for phenol degradation are analyzed by pseudo first kinetic model [49,66]. The rate constant equation for this model is given as:
(2)
kt = l n (
C t
C o
)
where “Co” is the initial concentration of phenol, and “Ct” is the concentration of phenol after time “t”.

Based on this equation rate constants calculated are shown in Fig. 8c. As observed 0.5GCW sample exhibited best degradation performance, it shows the maximum rate constant of 0.0053 min−1 amongst all samples synthesized.

The investigation of the reactive species (radical test) for phenol degradation is also performed by addition IPA and NaI in phenol solution before degradation, functioning as ·OH radicals and holes scavengers, respectively. The results obtained are shown in Fig. 9 , it can be observed that when IPA is added, the phenol degradation is significantly decreased which indicating the scavenging of ·OH radicals and restricting phenol degradation. On contrary the addition of NaI the phenol degradation for holes scavenging, not significantly impacting the phenol degradation. Based on the obtained results from scavenging experiments, it can be proposed that ·OH radicals are the prime mover for the phenol degradation and holes(h+) and superoxide radicals (O2 ·- radicals) contributed in a minor extent to its degradation.

The performance enhancement of the composite samples after composting with GQDs can be explained based on optical properties mainly PL spectra. As it is obvious form PL spectra that addition of GQDs, PL emission peak corresponding to CuWO4 quenched toa greater extent in the composite samples which indicates the efficient drainage of photogenerated charges form the CuWO4 to the GQDs conductive pathways. Further a mild increase of absorbance in the visible range of composite samples, 0.5GCW sample, as compared to pure CuWO4. Such an enhanced absorbance may lead to enhanced photogeneration of charges, thus influencing the photocatalytic performance. Based on the reported literature, various reactions involved in the photocatalytic degradation of phenol are presented in eq.3–9, and schematic view is displayed in Fig. 10 . When photocatalyst is illuminated by incident light with an appropriate wavelength, photogenerated electrons jump in the conduction band while holes are created in the valence band. The photogenerated charges would interact with water and oxygen (absorbed on the material surface) and generate free radicals such as OH• and O2 –.

Visible light active composite photocatalysts comprising of CuWO4 and GQDs were successfully synthesized by simple and facile synthesis approach. The photocatalytic performance of the composite photocatalysts was evaluated via degradation of phenol simulated wastewater. Various composite photocatalysts were prepared by varying the amount of CuWO4 photocatalyst vs GQDs solution for the aim of achieving optimized sample. It was observed that 0.5GCW sample exhibits the best photocatalytic performance. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of CuWO4/GQDs samples as compared to pure CuWO4 samples is associated with the optimized GQDs content leading to inhibition of electron-hole recombination and mild enhancement of absorbance within visible range. Hence, the addition of cheap additives oriented toward the formation of composites can be an efficient and cost-effective approach towards the improved activity of wastewater treatment. These results provide an experimental basis to further explore the pathway of phenol degradation and to develop visible light active photocatalysts for environmental remediation.

Woo Young Kim: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Project administration, Resources.
